content: Hello everyone, I am a PhD student majoring in information security. My professor wants me to participate in the RFL project and i would like to receive some assignments to strengthen my familiarity with the project (i am new to linux). I have tried to write a tty0tty driver, although it may not be good and there are many changes that need to be made. Now I would like to get another task (preferably for a simple drive device too, because too complex a drive device may be too difficult for me), any suggestions?

By the way, I have attached my tty0tty work here, hoping that you can give some suggestions on this.
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/pull/1090
I notice that your tty0tty work is based on the deprecated rust branch. Have you read through our website? https://rust-for-linux.com/

I understand that the rust branch is deprecated, but I implemented tty0tty using many abstractions not found in the Rust-Next branch. I'm trying to migrate the tty0tty module to the rust-next branch, but I'd also like to get involved in some new driver writing projects(maybe not so complex), any recommendations?
Also, no one reviews the rust branch anymore, right? So it's not going to marge the new PR?

The main barrier for getting involved is that to get a driver merged, it must be a driver that the relevant Linux kernel maintainer actually wants. Most of the time, this means that it needs to be a new driver that the kernel doesn't already have. I don't have a suggestion for a driver, unfortunately.

As for your PR, sending PRs on github isn't how code gets merged. When Rust was accepted into the kernel in ... I think it was late 2022, we switched to using the Linux kernel mailing list instead of github.
There are some good-first-issues on github that you are welcome to look at. They are about fixing existing issues, rather than adding a new driver, though.
@wenzhao Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing for some tips and ideas on how to contribute, depending on what you want to do.

Alice Ryhl said:

As for your PR, sending PRs on github isn't how code gets merged. When Rust was accepted into the kernel in ... I think it was late 2022, we switched to using the Linux kernel mailing list instead of github.

A member(@Trevor Gross ) suggested that I submit a PR before sending an email to let people know what I did. I also worry that bad work creates unnecessary headaches for linux reviewers :(

While I am not primarily concerned with whether my driver is merged, my main goal is to gain a deeper understanding of Rust and Linux through my involvement in the Rust for Linux project. As a doctoral student exploring future research directions, this experience is invaluable to me :)

I suggested the PR just as a place where it's easy for someone to give you some general feedback without noise on the list - merging PRs doesn't happen anymore. And as the others have stated (and I previously mentioned), everything that gets merged needs an in-tree usecase and really can't be a duplicate of a C driver. In other words, abstractions really can't be merged if the only thing they support is sample code.

I'll still take a look at the PR once I get the chance, just to help you get an idea of the shape things usually take. Which I think is all you are looking for. Weren't you considering making it work on top of rust-next?

As far as what to do next, I don't really know of anything one-off projects that have a chance of getting upstreamed (though you could look around the non-rust lists and see if you anything interesting). If you are interested in tty, maybe you could do work getting the relevant abstractions up to date so that they work on top of rust-next? Presumably they'll be needed by something at some point
